This truly is an amazing job. Yet another thing I vaguely read about but never had the chance to delve into. Thanks.

I'm reading https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/stable.html. It poses some important considerations and suggests to test all minor Python versions. According to psutil's `setup.py` the most minor support Python version is 3.4:

```python
    if setuptools is not None:
        kwargs.update(
            python_requires=">=2.7, !=3.0.*, !=3.1.*, !=3.2.*, !=3.3.*",
            extras_require=extras_require,
            zip_safe=False,
        )
```

As such I wonder: 
* would it make sense for `build.yml` to test 3.4? 
* what python versions are we testing right now?  I see that windows has `["3.7", "3.8", "3.9", "3.10", "~3.11.0-0"]` but I'm unclear about the other platforms.

Again, thanks for this great work!

Just one clarification for an unrelated change sneaked in this PR:

Overall it's good, but I have some comment about ExecutionResult.

The issue about ExecutionResult is that the semantic of this one actually are not clear.

Here it's supposed to be treated as a data structure, but it also has business logic which makes things complicated.

I think the direction is good, that we are abstracting the way function get executed for actor reader/writer and normal functions. So should we call it StepExecutor and do this:

```
e = StepExecutor(step_type, None, None, catch_exceptions)

    from ray.experimental.workflow.common import WorkflowStatus
    for _ in range(step_max_retries):
        try:
            workflow_context.update_workflow_step_context(context, step_id)
            args, kwargs = _resolve_step_inputs(step_inputs)
            # in side run, it'll do:
            # wrap_run/commit/run_sub/unpack
            ret = ret.run(func, *args, **kwargs)
            break
        except BaseException as e:
            error = e

    if step_type != StepType.READONLY_ACTOR_METHOD:
        if catch_exceptions or error is None
            _record_step_status(step_id, WorkflowStatus.FINISHED)
        else:
            _record_step_status(step_id, WorkflowStatus.RESUMABLE)
           raise error

    if catch_exceptions:
        return (error, ret)
    else:
        return ret
```

Nice. Could you please copy those syntax examples from the design doc into the PR description? I think it will help future people who `git blame` to understand this change.

Also, I'm not following why parameters come before the generated part? Is that for parity with URLs, like Benjy mentioned? I'm still trying to reason through how target generators will interact with parametrization, e.g. if `overrides` is legal:

```python
python_sources(
   overrides={"utils.py": {"resolves": parametrize("a", "b")}},
)
```

I hope that is, `overrides` is important for our goal of precise metadata. 

This example speaks to why my intuition would think the parameters come _after_ the generated target. We go from least specific to most specific. The parameters are variants of ~the same target, they get more specific.

--

If im not wrong, the Message consumer runs as a single thread and in turn creates multiple processor threads. Are you sure that by catching the interrupt here, all the processor threads are also stopped? This is of course assuming that ctrl+c is intended to stop the server.
Thank you. You are right, I was not ensuring that the consumer and its thread pool stopped. I have added the consumer.stop call which will stop the thread pool. Thank you!

Leaving aside the organization of the files (`cpp` files shouldn't be in `include`, I expect? I don't know how other projects do this), the build system (luckily we have @henryiii for this, willing to help out, so I'm not worried about this :-) ), and some bikeshedding on names (`PYBIND11_DECLARATIONS_ONLY` is better, but still not a fan; if I think of something better, I'll shout, but this is not urgent in the current context), I think this all looks pretty good :-)

The more I'm making PRs and compiling (and running) pybind11's test suite, the more I think I really want this PR to go in ;-)

The one thing we should still discuss is what inline declarations are still fine to keep in the headers. I'm thinking of empty constructors (maybe they should become `= default;` actually), or things as simple as `setValue(int value) { m_value = value; }`. Also in non-header-only projects, these are often left in the definition of a class in the header, no? So it would be worth testing out how much they influence the time it takes to compile; I'm hoping it'd be negligible.

@Frassle @RobbieMcKinstry thank you for your (very prompt!) feedback!

Based on @Frassle's comment I modified this to no longer set defaults during import etc. Now we'll _only_ set Created+Updated during a create, and Updated during an update. With this behavior it's possible and very likely for a resource to have Updated set with a nil Created. This is reasonable if we expect these to represent an exact timestamp and not a best guess.

I took a stab at a lifecycle test that goes through create → refresh → update steps. The timestamps _appear_ to propagate correctly but I feel uncertain. Is there some pathological case to consider that could accidentally set these to nil? Do reads matter? Failed operations? 🤔 

Folks using older CLI releases will of course reset these to nil -- that's unavoidable, and also a problem that lessens over time as people upgrade. We _might_ be able to detect this situation on the SaaS and generate some server-side timestamps, but I haven't looked into it yet.

I think I got ahead of myself trying to DRY things up with the `useCopies` business. FWIW - the thing I like about it is that it is an encapsulation of a piece of logic with a simple API to do the things that this specific instance would need. It's like a microservice for storing a piece of data that one component uses without having to wire up props and events and their accompanying methods and local state. CopiesModal reads the state and acts based on it, other components can write to the state when they want the CopiesModal to be visible. In any case - I definitely see how the indirection here is overkill for the problem at hand.

The thought re: a mixin is less about "global state" and more about just making all of that stuff, the props, events, handlers, local state live in one place that can be mixed in wherever the `CopieModal` component would be used - but even here this is only used in a couple places and I think I'd rather have duplicated logic between components than a mixin in this case.

Thank you all for the feedback - I am glad I tried something that is relatively new to me and for your critical eyes on it :) 

---

I'll be following up on this PR tomorrow and pushing changes hopefully midday tomorrow as I don't think it'll take too much time to clean up the tests per Misha's comments and to move the CopiesModal business into the components.

Arguably this removes the distinction between treatment of a single type and a 1-element union when using a discriminator.

Presumably this would (very slightly) negatively impact performance for users who use this, because `pydantic_core` would first validate the tag and then prodeed to validate the whole type (which would include the tag again) - i.e. it's not an optimization in the one-member case, unlike for multiple members.

An argument to not do this would be that by forbidding it we don't give users the opportunity to deoptimize themselves. Users can already handle the case of single members differently in the examples @dmontagu illustrated above.

Overall this seems like a tradeoff of usability versus performance. I think because the error (of trying to use `discriminant` for a non-union type, i.e. single-element union) fails at model build time, I'm currently falling just on the side of not allowing this.

We could benchmark the performance impact; maybe the overhead added is irrelevant, in which case I'd be👍

Speaking of yarn invocations, [apparently](https://yarnpkg.com/features/zero-installs) with Yarn 2+ it's... well, _debatably_ a good idea, but at the very least "not wrong" (according to **them**, anyway) to check into the repo either:

1. The Yarn version in use, so that everyone is running the same tools, or
2. The _entire_ dependency cache, iff it's in the form of compressed package archives rather than a giant, exploded `node_modules` hierarchy. That requires switching to using [their `pnp` linker](https://yarnpkg.com/features/pnp), instead of the `node-modules` one that's the default for projects coming from yarn 1.

Not necessities by any stretch, but apparently options possibly worth considering.

This is way easier to implement than I had thought! Heh.

There's one case that this patch fails to handle correctly. It's when a parent directory doesn't have a gitignore but the child directory does. Git respects the child's gitignore but this implementation does not :(

```console
~/programming/testing/gitignore on master via v3.8.5 (black) 
❯ tree -a --matchdirs -I .git
.
├── a.py
└── some-dir
    ├── b.py
    └── .gitignore

1 directory, 3 files

~/programming/testing/gitignore on master via v3.8.5 (black) 
❯ cat some-dir/.gitignore 
*.py

~/programming/testing/gitignore on master via v3.8.5 (black) 
❯ git clean -xfn
Would remove some-dir/b.py  # <-- git respects the child's gitignore

~/programming/testing/gitignore on master via v3.8.5 (black) 
❯ black . --check -v
.git ignored: matches the --exclude regular expression
a.py already well formatted, good job.
some-dir/b.py already well formatted, good job.  # < -- but not Black :/
All done! ✨ 🍰 ✨
2 files would be left unchanged.
```

This shouldn't be hard to fix but it will make the patch not as amazingly simple haha :)

This will also require changes to the [.gitignore documentation we currently have](https://black.readthedocs.io/en/stable/usage_and_configuration/file_collection_and_discovery.html#gitignore). If you don't want to handle this part, I wouldn't mind writing the docs!

I'm also starting to really dislike the file collection / discovery tests we have. They need a fair bit of boilerplate and are just annoying to read. This is obviously out of scope for this PR, but I wanted to get it out there. 

Thank you for sticking through with this feature! I'm looking forward to when this is merged.

```python
from sklearn.datasets import make_regression


def _create_data(objective, n_samples=100, n_features=None, centers=2, output='array', chunk_size=50, random_weights=True):
    n_features = n_features if n_features else 100
    X, y = make_regression(n_samples=n_samples, n_features=n_features, random_state=42)
    rnd = np.random.RandomState(42)
    weights = rnd.random(X.shape[0]) * 0.01
    if not random_weights:
        weights = np.ones([X.shape[0]])
    dX = da.from_array(X, (chunk_size, X.shape[1]))
    dy = da.from_array(y, chunk_size)
    dw = da.from_array(weights, chunk_size)

    return X, y, weights, dX, dy, dw


def _r2_score(dy_true, dy_pred):
    numerator = ((dy_true - dy_pred) ** 2).sum(axis=0, dtype=np.float64)
    denominator = ((dy_true - dy_pred.mean(axis=0)) ** 2).sum(axis=0, dtype=np.float64)
    return (1 - numerator / denominator).compute()


def _accuracy_score(dy_true, dy_pred):

eval_names_prefix = 'specified'

n_eval_sets = len(eval_sizes)
early_stopping_rounds = 1
eval_set = []
eval_sample_weight = []

eval_names = [eval_names_prefix + f'_{i}' for i in range(len(eval_sizes))]

eval_metrics = ['rmse']

for eval_size in eval_sizes:

full_trees = 100

assert dask_model.best_iteration_ < full_trees

p1_r2 = _r2_score(dy, p1)
msg = f'r2 score of predictions with actuals was <= 0.8 ({p1_r2})'

# check that evals_result contains expected eval_set names when provided.
n_rounds_tried = dask_model.best_iteration_ + early_stopping_rounds

assert len(evals_result) == n_eval_sets
evals_result_names = list(evals_result.keys())
```

**A Theory**

I have a theory about what's going on. I think that it might be the case that once early stopping has been detected, workers are shutting down and not carefully coordinating the shutdown. That could create a race condition where the order that they shut down matters. I suspect this because you were getting `socket recv error: 104`.

That comes from https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/e5c3f7e755659c120b94c109b515569fa6765b9c/src/network/socket_wrapper.hpp#L286, and the specific integer value comes from https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/e5c3f7e755659c120b94c109b515569fa6765b9c/src/network/socket_wrapper.hpp#L168. I saw answers on Stack Overflow saying that 104 means connection reset by peer, and based on https://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9699919799/basedefs/errno.h.html I can confirm that's true. You could run the code below on http://cpp.sh/ (or locally with the C++ compiler of your choice) to confirm.

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <cmath>
#include <cerrno>
#include <cstring>
#include <clocale>
#include <cstdio>
 
int main()
{
    std::cout << "message: " << std::strerror(ECONNRESET) << '\n';
    printf("error code: %d\n", ECONNRESET);
}
```

> message: Connection reset by peer
error code: 104

I think this type of problem could explain all three behaviors you're seeing:

* sometimes it works
* sometimes training fails with this "connection reset by peer"
* sometimes it hangs forever

We could try to figure this out by looking at how [mmlspark](https://github.com/Azure/mmlspark) handles early stopping, and by `@`-ing in other maintainers here. We could also try to see if the problem shows up in non-Dask distributed training, like with the LightGBM CLI, by picking up #3841.

HOWEVER, before we do that, I have a request.

**A Request**

Can you open a separate PR with only the non-early-stopping eval changes? Being able to use evaluation sets is valuable even without early stopping, because that allows you to get a deeper understanding of how the training process is progressing. The `test_eval_set_without_early_stopping` tests passed every time I ran them, so I'm fairly confident that they could be separated out.

```shell
pytest test_dask.py::test_eval_set_without_early_stopping
```

Doing the evaluation sets separately would also make this PR smaller and make it a bit easier for us to focus in on the source of instability uncovered by your tests.

Thanks! Yamllint support sounds great :)

Thoughts on not adding a target type for this, and instead having it operate on any YAML file? We have less precedent for that because the technology is newer, but it's a direction we're interesting in moving more towards. 

My main concern with targets is the intersection with `files` vs `resources` -- usually you'd use those targets to describe YAML files and add them as `dependencies` to your other targets like `python_sources`. That's going to be necessary to keep doing, and the backends like Python won't do anything when you add a `yaml_source` target to their `dependencies` because they only add `file` and `resource`.

Another benefit of not using targets is it's less boilerplate.

The only thing we don't have totally figured out is how to handle telling Pants to skip linting certain YAML files. Normally, we have the `skip_my_tool` field on individual targets, like `skip_flake8`. That can be nice because it's decentralized. Here, the only idea I have is the option `[yammlint].skip_globs`. It should work out well, only is a difference that it's now a centralized list rather than decentralized in BUILD files.

What do you think? 

Here's an example of a file-less linter, and I'm happy to give better instructions if you agree with this path:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/afd41699a791208b785a893e1687950b2a0f2f36/src/python/pants/backend/project_info/regex_lint.py#L258-L342

@ericl @jianoaix In general, consistently resetting the index was a very early design decision, where we decided that a block's index should represent the dataset record order; this means that we need to reset the index whenever we slice, concatenate, or shuffle blocks, with the end-result being that each block is locally ordered, and if all blocks are concatenated together (e.g. via `ds.to_pandas()`), the resulting index would give the global dataset order. This was under the assumption that the user would _not_ be encoding application-level identifiers into the index, since we hadn't encountered that use case at the time.

@jianoaix This particular index drop was added because of this issue: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/27019/commits/a48e34d25477f4d0f90b7bb7ac4909c8a042b696#r931308133.

If we're going to try to maintain the index, we can't only make the change on concatenation; we would need to do a global audit of all other places we reset the index, i.e. slicing, taking, shuffling:
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/055c2e4a962ab6afd418886eb55b5992a59dd25b/python/ray/data/_internal/pandas_block.py#L147
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/055c2e4a962ab6afd418886eb55b5992a59dd25b/python/ray/data/_internal/pandas_block.py#L154
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/055c2e4a962ab6afd418886eb55b5992a59dd25b/python/ray/data/_internal/pandas_block.py#L167

And before we decide to try to maintain the index, we need to ensure that index-aware operations (i.e. operations that automatically align on indices) will not now give incorrect results, and we might want to only preserve the original index if the index is not the default sequential ordering index (assuming there's an easy way to check that).

> The thing here that I don't think it is ok is the upgrade behavior and tests, so would love the feedback! Most specifically, I don't think that I should be calling `cparser.llhttp_resume_after_upgrade(self._cparser)`, but expose this as a method like `def resume_after_upgrade(self)`, but left it there to hear your opinion.

Looks like what `llhttp_resume_after_upgrade()` does is quite simple - it reset the parser's internal error state if it was paused for upgrade. So according to another doc you quoted:

> ```
>  * NOTE: if this function ever returns a non-pause type error, it will continue
>  * to return the same error upon each successive call up until `llhttp_init()`
>  * is called.
> llhttp_errno_t llhttp_execute(llhttp_t* parser, const char* data, size_t len);
> ```

It's all about how the parser behaves after detecting an upgrade. In short, I think you're right to call `llhttp_resume_after_upgrade()` in `feed_data()` (the only missing step in your PR is to calculate the pointer's difference, see below), or successive calls to `feed_data()` will raise `HttpParserUpgrade` errors, which is inconsistent with the current released httptools. What's more, according to the docs of `llhttp_get_error_pos()`:

> Returns the pointer to the last parsed byte before the returned error. The
> pointer is relative to the `data` argument of `llhttp_execute()`.
> 
> Note: this method might be useful for counting the number of parsed bytes.

There's no guarantee that the 2nd call to `feed_data()` is using the same `data` parameter - in other words, the return value of `llhttp_get_error_pos()` is considered an invalid pointer without the context of the first call to `feed_data()`, so we should calculate the number of parsed bytes while we can.

For the behavior of calling `feed_data()` again after handling `HttpParserUpgrade` errors, we should add a test for it, even though this is a rare use case.

TL;DR: Please see suggested patch below:

```patch
diff --git a/httptools/parser/parser.pyx b/httptools/parser/parser.pyx
index 6656bef..3158d0e 100644
--- a/httptools/parser/parser.pyx
+++ b/httptools/parser/parser.pyx
@@ -161,13 +161,15 @@ cdef class HttpParser:
     def feed_data(self, data):
         cdef:
             size_t data_len
-            cparser.llhttp_errno_t nb
+            cparser.llhttp_errno_t err
             Py_buffer *buf
+            bint owning_buf = False
+            char* err_pos

         if PyMemoryView_Check(data):
             buf = PyMemoryView_GET_BUFFER(data)
             data_len = <size_t>buf.len
-            nb = cparser.llhttp_execute(
+            err = cparser.llhttp_execute(
                 self._cparser,
                 <char*>buf.buf,
                 data_len)
@@ -175,21 +177,34 @@ cdef class HttpParser:
         else:
             buf = &self.py_buf
             PyObject_GetBuffer(data, buf, PyBUF_SIMPLE)
+            owning_buf = True
             data_len = <size_t>buf.len

-            nb = cparser.llhttp_execute(
+            err = cparser.llhttp_execute(
                 self._cparser,
                 <char*>buf.buf,
                 data_len)

-            PyBuffer_Release(buf)
-
-        if self._cparser.upgrade == 1 and nb == cparser.HPE_PAUSED_UPGRADE:
-            read_bytes = cparser.llhttp_get_error_pos(self._cparser)
-            cparser.llhttp_resume_after_upgrade(self._cparser)
-            raise HttpParserUpgrade(read_bytes)
-
-        if nb != cparser.HPE_OK:
+        try:
+            if self._cparser.upgrade == 1 and err == cparser.HPE_PAUSED_UPGRADE:
+                err_pos = cparser.llhttp_get_error_pos(self._cparser)
+
+                # Immediately free the parser from "error" state, simulating
+                # http-parser behavior here because 1) we never had the API to
+                # allow users manually "resume after upgrade", and 2) the use
+                # case for resuming parsing is very rare.
+                cparser.llhttp_resume_after_upgrade(self._cparser)
+
+                # The err_pos here is specific for the input buf. So if we ever
+                # switch to the llhttp behavior (re-raise HttpParserUpgrade for
+                # successive calls to feed_data() until resume_after_upgrade is
+                # called), we have to store the result and keep our own state.
+                raise HttpParserUpgrade(err_pos - <char*>buf.buf)
+        finally:
+            if owning_buf:
+                PyBuffer_Release(buf)
+
+        if err != cparser.HPE_OK:
             ex = parser_error_from_errno(
                 self._cparser,
                 <cparser.llhttp_errno_t> self._cparser.error)
diff --git a/tests/test_parser.py b/tests/test_parser.py
index 101da22..cd6f59c 100644
--- a/tests/test_parser.py
+++ b/tests/test_parser.py
@@ -332,11 +332,11 @@ class TestRequestParser(unittest.TestCase):

             p.feed_data(UPGRADE_REQUEST1)
         except httptools.HttpParserUpgrade as ex:
-            offset = len(ex.args[0])

         else:
             self.fail('HttpParserUpgrade was not raised')

-        self.assertEqual(UPGRADE_REQUEST1[-offset:], b'Hot diggity dogg')
+        self.assertEqual(UPGRADE_REQUEST1[offset:], b'Hot diggity dogg')

         self.assertEqual(headers, {
             b'Sec-WebSocket-Key2': b'12998 5 Y3 1  .P00',
@@ -347,6 +347,11 @@ class TestRequestParser(unittest.TestCase):
```

The old behavior was `axis=0`, which is a default some functions have (`ak.concatenate`, `ak.unflatten`, and `ak.is_none`, which is perhaps the most relevant for comparison with `ak.fill_none`).

I see some value in making the default `axis=None` since that's what people have sometimes expected (I remember some old issues about that), but I don't think the expectation is strong enough to warrant the pain of a deprecation cycle. If someone asks, "Why didn't it get the nested `Nones`?" we can just say, "Use `axis=None`" and that would probably be the end of the conversation. The documentation for this function should have examples with `axis=some int` and `axis=None` for users to hopefully notice.

So if you're asking me to make an executive decision, I'll do it: make the default `axis` of this function `0`, and then the old tests will work without modification.

Has the `axis=None` case not been implemented? I don't see it here or in the tests. Would that be hard to add (because `recursively_apply` has to be applied to the output of an `apply`)?

Hi @mattwwarren! Thank you for this. I've gone through and noted a few issues that would need to be addressed before landing, but the central issue is handling of the `application/form-data`.

Looking at #289 again I think that I may have misinterpreted what you were after. Did you want to _set the Content-Type header of an application/x-www-form-urlencoded request to application/form-data_? That doesn't make sense, as the server will fail to parse it due to the content-type mismatch, won't it? Or did you want to generate an application/form-data request without actual files? The latter is actually supported — you can do `treq.post(..., files=[('field', 'value'), ...])` (or even pass a `dict` to *files*). You could also accomplish this by passing a `MultiPartProducer()` instance as the *data* parameter, along with the matching Content-Type header.

I'm left wondering if this is actually a documentation issue (of which, admittedly, there are many). The ability to override the Content-Type of a JSON request still seems useful to me, but it's a smaller feature than what you've implemented here.

Again, thank you for the PR! I'd love to work with you to make treq work for your use case.

I have got a couple of things that I would like to see addressed that I believe should be pretty easy to do and leave the code in better shape. I don't want this to be a blocker though, so that's why I'm giving my approval. These recommendations are optional, and you can do what you want with them.

First, I think we should organize our `plugins` module so that it has room to grow. This can be accomplished by adding a sub-module `virtual_packages` to organize all the new files you added (e.g. `cuda.py`). After doing this, the directory structure would look like this:

```
./plugins
├── __init__.py
└── virtual_packages
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── archspec.py
    ├── cuda.py
    ├── linux.py
    ├── osx.py
    └── windows.py
```

Second, I think we should have a special `register_virtual_packages` function. Perhaps this could live in `conda/plugins/virtual_packages/__init__.py`? I'd like to see all the `pm.register` calls moved there. Here's what that would look like:

```python
def register_virtual_packages(pm: PluginManager) -> None:
   """
   Please add a nice doc string too explaining why this function exists!
   """
    pm.register(archspec)
    pm.register(cuda)
    pm.register(linux)
    pm.register(osx)
    pm.register(windows)
```

This would later be called and imported in the `get_plugin_manager` function.

Also, I think that you should mention something in the documentation about the following virtual package names being reserved:

- archspec
- cuda
- unix
- linux
- libc (more maybe?)
- win
- osx

If someone were to implement their own version of those, it would error out right? That should definitely be documented. It's okay not to do this now, but please make a ticket to do so in the future.

Also, the documentation tells me how to create a virtual package plugin, but I'm not necessary sold on why or when I might need to do this. I'm guessing we're still trying to figure this out ourselves, but let's make sure to come back to this piece of documentation in the future to present a more compelling use case.

That's it for now.

Mostly LGTM, the big thing that we need resolve IMO is the semi-lazy read behavior. Semi-lazy reading (eagerly reading the first block/file) has been confusing for users, results in redundant reading when there's stage fusion or conversion to a pipeline, and I think we should try to get rid of it when switching to lazy execution by default, if possible. I think that it would be much better if we had fully lazy mode and eager mode, where semi-lazy execution is an execution optimization that is enabled when we determine we only need to compute a subset of blocks; these semantics should translate pretty well to our new execution planner and streaming execution model.

Instead of always computing the first block, I think that we should make the read fully lazy, and if the user immediately calls `ds.schema()` right after reading, we only then trigger reading of the first block (and only if the schema isn't already available from e.g. file metadata).

I _think_ it should be pretty straightforward to move this logic to the `ExecutionPlan`, since we can have `ExecutionPlan.schema()` (and other ops that only require the first block) trigger semi-lazy execution with e.g. a certain flag set, and if that flag is set AND the plan only consists of a read stage AND the input blocks are a lazy block list, we trigger minimal computation of that read stage.

Hmm I feel like it is not a good idea to query all drivers. This pattern has not been working well in the past too for RPC that require reliable response time. There are 3 cons; 

- it introduces a new code path when we already have an aggregation path everyone knows (resource loads). 
- I believe GetAllJobInfo is called frequently which can add unnecessary loads to user drivers.
- It can be common for the driver to be overloaded sometimes. In this case, GetAllJobInfo APIs will become extremely slow. If 1 driver is slow to respond. If the driver exits unexpectedly, it can take up to keepalive timeout (30~60 seconds) to detect the failure. 
 
We are reporting every autoscaler-related data via periodic aggregation, and is there any special reason why we make a special case only to this field? Is it possible to instead

1. when raylets send loads to GCS, we include the # of pending tasks there.
2. And just store them to GCS, and use it when replying GetAllJobInfo?

I am not entirely sure what you want to achieve when using artifacts. If I understand correctly, you use artifacts as a way to store the result of the build, with a key based on the package version listed in the `setup.py` file. However, relying on the version identifier seems very risky: while we are on a working branch, the same version identifier might be used with different dependencies sets.
If I understood your intention correctly, I recommend to instead use the [cache action](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-workflows/caching-dependencies-to-speed-up-workflows#using-the-cache-action) with a `hashFiles('setup.py')` key instead. This will save you the hassle of managing directories, as the `cache` action will always load and restore to the same, and will make sure that the cache is invalidated if the _contents_ of `setup.py` change 🙂

IIUC, we are only using `.as_subclass(torch.Tensor)` here to avoid `__torch_function__` here, correct? If yes, why not simply use the context manager that disables this?

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/07ae61bf9c21ddd1d5f65d326aa9636849b383ca/torchvision/prototype/features/_feature.py#L102-L103

like

```py
with DisableTorchFunction():
    return self.shape
```

Better yet, instead of adding properties for all these attributes manually, why not overwrite `__getattribute__` to do this for us?

```py
    _NO_TORCH_FUNCTION = {
        "shape",
    }

    def __getattribute__(self, item):
        with DisableTorchFunction() if item in type(self)._NO_TORCH_FUNCTION else contextlib.nullcontext():
            return object.__getattribute__(self, item)
```

Test:

```py
import unittest.mock

import torch
from torchvision.prototype import features

image = features.Image(torch.rand(3, 16, 16))

with unittest.mock.patch(
    "torchvision.prototype.features._Feature.__torch_function__",
    side_effect=AssertionError,
):
    image.shape
```

I have not benchmarked this. If it is as fast or faster than your suggestion, I would prefer this for several reasons:

1. We don't need to add properties manual, which is harder to maintain.
2. Subclasses can extend `_NO_TORCH_FUNCTION` or whatever we call it to also avoid `__torch_function__` calls on the metadata they are adding, i.e. `Image.color_space`.
3. Since we don't need to unwrap anymore, we can also avoid the issues @datumbox mentioned in https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/6681#discussion_r986001975.

I'm comfortable using `RD` in the `wch1/r-debug1` image because:

1. We're only creating a _source_ package, so I'm not worried about an alternative R build generating different artifacts.
2. The package will be tested against `r-devel` when uploaded to CRAN anyway, so there's no risk of "created a package using a non-standard version of R that breaks things on a CRAN submission".

I pulled the artifact created from this PR's most recent CI run (https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/4811#issuecomment-973079004) and ran `R CMD check --as-cran` on it, just on my Mac (R 4.1.1 from CRAN, macOS 11.14). No issues found.
